mnemonics.title: Budget Spending Traps
mnemonics.content:
U - Unspent funds forfeited
S - Spending pressure at year-end
E - Emergency purchases
I - Impulse buying increases
L - Lost future allocations
I - Inefficient resource use
T - Time-wasting decisions
O - Over-budgeting results
R - Rush decisions made
L - Low-value purchases
O - Overlooked strategic needs
S - Savings lost forever
E - Expenses misaligned
I - Investment planning suffers

acronyms.usage: Long-term infrastructure investments like servers, data centers, network equipment
Ongoing costs like cloud subscriptions, licenses, maintenance contracts, utilities
Measure of the value generated from IT spending relative to cost
Complete cost of acquiring and operating an IT asset over its lifecycle
Framework for designing, delivering, managing IT services aligned with business needs

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circular flow showing: Planning → Allocation → Spending → Review → Adjustment. Show how 'Use It or Lose It' creates a broken loop at the spending stage.
Bar chart showing 80% of annual spending in Q4 (months 10-12), illustrating the artificial spike caused by fiscal year-end pressure.
Horizontal bar chart with center line at zero. Bars extending right = overspending (negative impact), bars extending left = underspending (risk to future budgets).
Two parallel lines - one for capacity needs (steady growth), one for budget (sawtooth pattern). Show how misalignment causes resource gaps or waste.

analogies.concept: Use It or Lose It budget
analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant buffet where you must fill your plate even if you're not hungry - you'll either waste food or overeat, neither is optimal.
Like packing an oversized suitcase 'just in case' - you pay for extra space you don't need but feel safer having it available.
Like grocery shopping when starving - you buy impulsively without planning, often getting things you don't need or won't use.
Like the minimum maintenance required for a car - change oil, check tires - skip it and the car eventually breaks down.
Like saving leftover vacation days for next year instead of losing them - you can use them strategically when needed.
